perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, such Financing will significantly benefit the Company and indirectly benefit the above-named Employee, as a stockholder of the Company; and WHEREAS, this Non-Disclosure, Assignment of Inventions and Non-Competition Agreement (this "Agreement") is a condition to the Investment and Stockholders' Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, such Financing will significantly benefit the Company; and WHEREAS, this Non-Disclosure, Assignment of Inventions and Non-Competition Agreement (this "Agreement") is a condition to the Investment and Stockholders' Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgement that the financing indirectly benefits the employee as a stockholder could, in some circumstances, weaken the consideration for the agreement, especially if challenged under DC law, which requires valid consideration for contracts. The implicit benefit to the employee helps justify the restrictive covenants.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 28:1-201 defines 'Agreement' as the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade. By removing the direct acknowledgement of indirect benefit, it weakens the argument that the restrictive covenants are supported by adequate consideration, potentially making the agreement unenforceable under District of Columbia contract law. The agreement needs consideration to be valid. The deleted text supports that the employee is getting something in return for signing, otherwise the agreement may be deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:1â201. General definitions. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:1â201. General definitions. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other articles of this subtitle that apply to particular articles or parts thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other articles of this subtitle that apply to particular articles or parts thereof: (1) âActionâ, in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, set-off, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) âAggrieved partyâ means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) âAgreementâ, as distinguished from âcontractâ, means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Â§ 28:1-303 . (4) âBankâ means a person engaged in the business of banking and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) âBearerâ means a person in control of a negotiable electronic document of title or a person in possession of a negotiable instrument, negotiable tangible document of title, or certificated security that is payable to bearer or indorsed in blank. (6) âBill of ladingâ means a document of title evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of directly or indirectly transporting or forwarding goods. The term does not include a warehouse receipt. (7) âBranchâ includes a separately incorporated foreign branch of a bank. (8) âBurden of establishingâ a fact means the burden of persuading the trier of fact that the existence of the fact is more probable than its nonexistence. (9) âBuyer in ordinary course of businessâ means a person that buys goods in good faith, without
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges the indirect benefit to the employee, which supports the validity of the Non-Disclosure, Assignment of Inventions and Non-Competition Agreement. The modified text removes this acknowledgement, potentially weakening the consideration for the agreement under District of Columbia contract law. The Law Explanation states that the agreement needs consideration to be valid. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Agreement' in D.C. Code § 28:1â201 as the bargain of the parties, including inferences from circumstances. Removing the acknowledgment weakens the inference of a bargain for the employee, thus contradicting the legal requirement for valid consideration.

perturbation.original_text: I will not at any time, whether during or after the termination of employment, for any reason whatsoever (other than to promote and advance the business of the Company), reveal to any person or entity (both commercial and non-commercial) or use for any purpose other than the furtherance of the Company's business interests any of the trade secrets or Confidential Information, including, but not limited to, the Company's research and development activities, marketing plans and strategies, pricing and costing policies, customer and supplier lists, and business or financial information of the Company so far as they have come or may come to my knowledge, except as may be required in the ordinary course of performing my duties as an employee of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: I will not at any time, whether during or after the termination of employment, for any reason whatsoever, reveal to any person or entity (both commercial and non-commercial) or use for any purpose any of the trade secrets or Confidential Information, including, but not limited to, the Company's research and development activities, marketing plans and strategies, pricing and costing policies, customer and supplier lists, and business or financial information of the Company so far as they have come or may come to my knowledge.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'other than to promote and advance the business of the Company' and 'other than the furtherance of the Company's business interests' and the clause 'except as may be required in the ordinary course of performing my duties as an employee of the Company' , the non-disclosure obligation becomes overly broad and could be interpreted to restrict the employee's ability to perform their job or to use general knowledge gained during their employment. This could violate the principle that non-compete and non-disclosure agreements must be reasonably tailored to protect legitimate business interests under DC law.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 32–571 effectively bans non-compete agreements for employees, stating that no employer shall require or request an employee to execute an agreement or comply with a workplace policy that prevents the employee from performing work for another person or entity, or operate their own business, during or after the employee's employment with the employer. While this is a non-disclosure, a broadly worded one could be construed as a de facto non-compete, which is outlawed. Removing the original allowance for actions that further company business, the restrictive clause becomes overly broad and possibly in violation of this code. This change introduces legal risk by making the clause potentially unenforceable due to its overbreadth, acting effectively as a non-compete.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: D.C. Law 24-335. Metro for D.C. Amendment Act of 2022. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu D.C. Law 24-335. Metro for D.C. Amendment Act of 2022. AN ACT To amend the Department of Transportation Establishment Act of 2002 to require the Director to enter into an agreement with the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority for fare-free Metrobus travel and expanded after-hours Metrobus service in the District, to establish the District Resident Transit Subsidy Program, to establish the Fare-Free Bus Service Fund, to establish the Bus Service Enhancement Fund, and to direct certain revenues to the Fare-Free Bus Service Fund and the Bus Service Enhancement Fund; and to amend section 47-1803.02 of the District of Columbia Official Code to exclude from gross income subsidies provided pursuant to the District Resident Transit Subsidy Program. BE IT ENACTED BY THE COUNCIL OF THE DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA, That this act may be cited as the "Metro for D.C. Amendment Act of 2022". Sec. 2. The Department of Transportation Establishment Act of 2002, effective May 21, 2002 (D.C. Law 14-137; D.C. Official Code Â§ 50-921.01 et seq. ), is amended as follows: Amend Â§ 50-921.01a (a) Section 2a (D.C. Official Code Â§ 50-921.01a) is amended as follows: (1) Paragraphs (1), (1A), and (1B) are, respectively, redesignated as paragraphs (1B), (1C), and (1D). (2) New paragraphs (1) and (1A) are added to read as follows: " (1) "DDOT" means the District Department of Transportation established by section 2 . " (1A) "Director" means head of DDOT appointed pursuant to section 3 .". Amend Â§ 50-921.02 (b) Section 3 (D.C. Official Code Â§ 50-921.02) is amended by adding a new subsection (h) to read as follows: " (h) (1) Within 60 days of the effective date of the Metro for D.C. Amendment Act of 2022, passed on 2nd reading on December 20, 2022 (Enrolled version of Bill 24-429) , the Director shall enter into a fare buydown agreement with the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority to facilitate: " (A) The provision of fare-free Metrobus transportation within the District; and " (B) Daily 24-hour Metrobus service on at least 12 Metrobus lines in the District, with a maximum of 20 minutes between any scheduled bus arrival at any stop. " (2) The Metrobus lines identified in subparagraph (B) of this paragraph shall be determined based on high ridership, with an emphasis on serving major transportation corridors and activity centers. " (3) The Director may amend the fare buydown agreement required by paragraph (1) of this subsection or enter in additional fare buydown agreements in order to expand the availability of Metrobus service within the District.". New subchapter V of Chapter 9A of Title 50 (b) A new title VI is added to read as follows: "TITLE VI. TRANSIT SUBSIDIES FOR DISTRICT RESIDENTS. New Â§ 50-921.81 " Sec. 11t. Definitions. " For the purposes of this title , the term: " (1) "DDOT" means the District Department of Transportation. " (2) "Participant" means an individual who has applied and been determined eligible for the Program within the prior 12 months, pursuant to section 11w . " (3) "Program" means the District Resident Transit Subsidy Program, established by section 11u . " (4) "WMATA" means the Washington Metropolitan Area Transit Authority. New Â§ 50-921.82 " Sec. 11u. Transit Subsidy Program established. " (a) There is established a District Resident Transit Subsidy Program, which shall be administered by DDOT, to provide subsidies to participants for travel on any transportation that accepts payment through a SmarTrip card issued by WMATA or a similar transit card utilized by WMATA or any other agency in the District or another jurisdiction with which the Mayor has entered into an agreement pursuant to subsection (b) of this section. " (b) The Mayor shall enter into an agreement with WMATA, and may enter into an agreement with any other agency in the District or another jurisdiction, to manage subsidies provided by the Program, i
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes exceptions that allowed the employee to reveal confidential information to promote the company's business interests or in the course of performing their duties. The Law Explanation states that under D.C. Code § 32–571, overly broad non-disclosure agreements can be seen as de facto non-competes, which are banned. By removing the allowances, the changed text creates a non-disclosure agreement that is overly broad, thereby potentially violating D.C. Code § 32–571.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that the restrictions set forth in this Section are intended to protect the Company's valid business interests and agree that such restrictions are reasonable and appropriate for this purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that the restrictions set forth in this Section are agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the statement about protecting 'valid business interests' and the agreement that the restrictions are 'reasonable and appropriate' weakens the enforceability of the non-compete and non-solicitation clauses. Under District of Columbia law, non-compete agreements must be reasonable in scope and necessary to protect legitimate business interests. The original language provides evidence that the employee acknowledged these factors. Removing it makes it more difficult for the Company to later argue that the restrictions are, in fact, reasonable and necessary.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 32–571 impacts the enforceability of agreements that restrain employees from working elsewhere or operating their own business. While the original text included a statement of reasonableness and business interest protection, removing this acknowledgment weakens the argument that the non-compete/non-solicitation is an exception to the ban, or narrowly tailored, thus, further pushing the agreement toward being non-compliant with current D.C. law. The previous statement added weight to the company's defense against claims of the restriction being in violation of labor laws.
perturbation.location: Section 4, NON-COMPETITION COVENANT and Section 5, NON-SOLICITATION

perturbation.justification: The modification omits the acknowledgment that the restrictions are 'reasonable and appropriate' and protect 'valid business interests.' The Law Explanation states that removing this acknowledgment weakens the argument that the non-compete/non-solicitation is narrowly tailored, thus further pushing the agreement toward being non-compliant with current D.C. law. The Perturbation Explanation also makes it clear that non-compete agreements must be reasonable in scope and necessary to protect legitimate business interests, and that removing the original language makes it more difficult for the Company to argue this. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly cover this issue, the explanations provided establish that this omission creates a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If I am terminated for any reason, in consideration for the Non-competition covenant and the other covenants and agreements set forth in the Agreement, I will receive a salary of $275,000 per annum for one year after such termination payable in bi-weekly installments in accordance with the Company's payroll procedures.
perturbation.changed_text: If I am terminated for any reason, I may receive severance.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for severance pay if the employee is terminated and describes its calculation. Removing the specific severance terms in exchange for the non-compete covenant creates uncertainty and could be seen as a lack of adequate consideration for the non-compete, especially given DC's stance on such clauses. This increases the risk of the non-compete being deemed unenforceable due to insufficient consideration.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Code § 32–571 places restrictions on the enforcement of non-compete agreements. By removing the specific severance terms, there is a lack of clarity if the non-compete covenant is supported by consideration which can impact the employee, and cause detriment, especially with the labor law's protection regarding the ability to compete. Without the assurance of a year's salary at termination, the non-compete may be viewed as unconscionable or lacking the necessary consideration to be enforceable, particularly in light of the DC's generally unfavorable view on non-compete clauses.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies severance pay upon termination in exchange for a non-compete agreement. The changed text replaces this with a vague statement about potentially receiving severance. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this creates uncertainty about consideration for the non-compete, making it potentially unenforceable under DC law. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that D.C. Code § 32–571 places restrictions on non-compete agreements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address non-compete agreements, the Perturbation and Law Explanations, coupled with the omission of specific severance terms, suggest the modified contract introduces a legal contradiction by potentially lacking adequate consideration for the non-compete, which is viewed unfavorably in DC.
